organic_virgin_coconut_oil.name: Organic Virgin Coconut Oil
organic_virgin_coconut_oil.mechanism: Cold-pressed virgin coconut oil retains its full lauric acid content — roughly 50% of its fatty acid profile. Lauric acid disrupts the lipid membrane of decay- and odor-causing bacteria, the same job synthetic antimicrobials do in commercial paste, without the irritation of detergent surfactants. Coconut oil also has natural surfactant properties that help loosen plaque and food particles during brushing.
organic_virgin_coconut_oil.sourcing: Cold-pressed and unrefined from organic coconuts. Never deodorized, never hydrogenated, never lab-stripped. The oil keeps its natural color, scent, and seasonal variation in fatty acid profile.
organic_virgin_coconut_oil.why_we_chose_it: Refined coconut oil is dramatically cheaper to formulate with — it's neutral, shelf-stable, and won't shift consistency at room temperature. We pay more for cold-pressed virgin because the lauric acid is the antimicrobial. The refining process strips out exactly the molecule that does the work. The texture variation customers notice between batches is a downstream cost of using oil that hasn't been stripped.
organic_virgin_coconut_oil.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: Refined, deodorized, or fractionated coconut oil. Lab-stripped of most lauric acid. Listed simply as 'coconut oil' on the back panel — looks identical to a customer reading the label, but it's a different molecule doing different work. Often hydrogenated in commercial pastes for shelf-stable consistency.

baking_soda.name: Baking Soda
baking_soda.mechanism: Sodium bicarbonate neutralizes the acidic oral environment that cavity-causing bacteria need to thrive — cavity formation requires a low-pH environment, and baking soda raises the pH. It also provides micro-fine abrasion that lifts surface stains (coffee, tea, wine) without scratching enamel. Its Mohs hardness is below that of tooth enamel; conventional abrasives like hydrated silica are above.
baking_soda.sourcing: Food-grade, aluminum-free sodium bicarbonate. The aluminum-free spec matters because some commercial baking sodas include aluminum-based anti-caking agents — not something we want anywhere near our customers' mouths.
baking_soda.why_we_chose_it: Hydrated silica is the cheap, common abrasive in mass-market toothpaste — easier to source in industrial volume and creates the gritty paste texture customers were trained to expect. But hydrated silica is harder than enamel; over years of daily use it wears down sensitive teeth. Baking soda is gentler and adds the pH-neutralization benefit, which silica doesn't have.
baking_soda.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: Hydrated silica, calcium carbonate, dicalcium phosphate dihydrate. All cheaper to source in industrial volume, all harder than enamel, all create the gritty paste consistency customers were trained to associate with cleaning. None offer the pH benefit.

xanthan_gum.name: Xanthan Gum
xanthan_gum.mechanism: A polysaccharide produced by fermentation of sugars by Xanthomonas campestris bacteria. It thickens water-based solutions into a gel without forming a film on surfaces. In our formula it gives the paste enough structure to stay on a toothbrush and dispense from the bottle, without the tooth-coating effect of glycerin or sorbitol.
xanthan_gum.sourcing: Non-GMO xanthan gum produced via standard fermentation. A common ingredient in natural-formula personal care and food applications.
xanthan_gum.why_we_chose_it: Glycerin and sorbitol are the cheap thickeners in conventional toothpaste — they create the paste consistency, add sweetness, and have humectant properties that help retain moisture. They also leave a coating on teeth that may interfere with natural remineralization. Xanthan gum gives the structure without the coating. Trade-off: our texture is closer to gel than paste.
xanthan_gum.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: Glycerin, sorbitol, propylene glycol. Sweet, cheap, effective at thickening — and effective at coating teeth. Glycerin appears in nearly every commercial toothpaste's ingredient list; it's the default solution to the 'how do you make a paste' problem.

wildcrafted_myrrh.name: Wildcrafted Myrrh
wildcrafted_myrrh.mechanism: Myrrh resin contains sesquiterpene compounds with documented anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activity. Particularly soothing for inflamed gum tissue — useful for customers with gingivitis, gum sensitivity, or post-dental-work irritation. Used in oral care for thousands of years across Egyptian, Greek, and traditional Chinese medicine traditions.
wildcrafted_myrrh.sourcing: Wildcrafted from Commiphora trees in their natural habitat — primarily the Horn of Africa and the Arabian Peninsula. Sustainably foraged from naturally occurring trees, not factory-farmed. Wildcrafted sourcing is harder to scale consistently and more expensive than cultivated alternatives.
wildcrafted_myrrh.why_we_chose_it: Most natural toothpastes skip myrrh entirely because it's expensive, difficult to source consistently, and adds complexity to formulation. Commercial alternatives substitute synthetic anti-inflammatories or skip the function entirely. We use it because it's traditional oral medicine that genuinely helps customers with reactive gums, and the customer who notices gum sensitivity decreasing after a few weeks knows what made the difference.
wildcrafted_myrrh.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: Either no anti-inflammatory ingredient at all, or a synthetic substitute like cetylpyridinium chloride (an antimicrobial in many mouthwashes and some toothpastes). Stannous fluoride is sometimes positioned as anti-inflammatory in the marketing of major brands. The customer with sensitive gums tends to know the difference within a week or two of switching.

stevia.name: Stevia
stevia.mechanism: Plant-derived sweetness from steviol glycosides extracted from the Stevia rebaudiana leaf. Non-cariogenic — does not feed the bacteria responsible for tooth decay the way sucrose does. Unlike sugar alcohols (xylitol, sorbitol) it has no laxative effect at incidental swallowed amounts. Sweet at very low concentrations, so a small amount goes a long way in formulation.
stevia.sourcing: Organic stevia leaf extract. We use a less-processed extract rather than the highly purified single-glycoside stevia common in food applications — slightly more bitter on the palate, more recognizable as a plant.
stevia.why_we_chose_it: Sodium saccharin and aspartame are dirt-cheap, shelf-stable, and intensely sweet — the default sweetener choice for commercial toothpaste. Xylitol is excellent for oral care because it actively inhibits cariogenic bacteria, but has its own taste profile and a higher per-unit cost than stevia. We chose stevia for taste, plant-derived sourcing, and ingredient-list legibility.
stevia.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: Sodium saccharin, sucralose, aspartame. Synthetic, shelf-stable for years, intensely sweet at trace amounts. Cheap to formulate with. Often the unspoken reason mass-market toothpaste tastes 'punch in the face' sweet — synthetic intensity, not flavor depth.

organic_jojoba.name: Organic Jojoba
organic_jojoba.mechanism: Jojoba is technically a wax ester, not an oil — its molecular structure mirrors human sebum closely enough that skin recognizes and absorbs it without forming an occlusive film. This makes it a fast-absorbing carrier that doesn't sit on top of skin the way mineral oil and petrolatum do. The same property keeps deodorant glide-on and lotion non-greasy.
organic_jojoba.sourcing: Cold-pressed from organic jojoba seeds (Simmondsia chinensis), grown primarily in the American Southwest and Mexico. Unrefined to retain the full wax-ester profile.
organic_jojoba.why_we_chose_it: Mineral oil and petrolatum are dramatically cheaper and form a more uniform film. They also block skin's own moisture exchange and can clog pores. Jojoba is the closest plant analog to sebum we know of, and the absorption behavior is the whole point of using it.
organic_jojoba.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: Mineral oil, petrolatum, dimethicone, or fractionated coconut oil. All cheaper, all form an occlusive film, all good at making a product feel like it's doing something while doing less than they appear to.

plant_emulsifying_wax.name: Plant-Based Emulsifying Wax
plant_emulsifying_wax.mechanism: Emulsifying wax holds water and oil phases together in a stable lotion or cream — without it, water and oil separate within hours. We use plant-derived emulsifying wax (typically from cetyl alcohol and vegetable-derived stearate). The wax forms micelles that suspend oil droplets in water and prevent phase separation through the product's shelf life.
plant_emulsifying_wax.sourcing: Plant-derived (vs. petroleum-derived). Vegetable cetearyl alcohol plus vegetable-derived polysorbate, blended for the emulsifier function. Non-GMO.
plant_emulsifying_wax.why_we_chose_it: PEG-based emulsifiers (polyethylene glycol esters) are the industrial standard — cheaper, more shelf-stable, and easier to formulate with. They're also synthetic and have known purity concerns (1,4-dioxane contamination). We pay more for a plant-derived emulsifier because it does the same job without the contamination question.
plant_emulsifying_wax.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: PEG-100 stearate, PEG-40 hydrogenated castor oil, ceteareth-20. All petroleum-derived, all do the emulsifier job, all carry the 1,4-dioxane contamination concern that requires the manufacturer to test for trace levels — something the consumer can't verify.

grapefruit_seed_extract.name: Organic Grapefruit Seed Extract
grapefruit_seed_extract.mechanism: Plant-derived antimicrobial extract that inhibits bacterial and fungal growth in water-containing formulas. Acts as a broad-spectrum natural preservative, extending shelf life of lotion, cream, and deodorant without parabens or phenoxyethanol. The active polyphenol fraction (naringenin and related flavonoids) does the antimicrobial work.
grapefruit_seed_extract.sourcing: Cold-pressed and concentrated from organic grapefruit seeds. Standardized for active polyphenol content.
grapefruit_seed_extract.why_we_chose_it: Parabens and phenoxyethanol are the cheap, shelf-stable preservatives that show up in nearly every commercial cosmetic. They work — and they're also the most-asked-about ingredients by customers reading labels. Grapefruit seed extract works as well in our formulas at the concentrations we use, and it's something a customer can pronounce.
grapefruit_seed_extract.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: Methylparaben, propylparaben, butylparaben, phenoxyethanol, methylisothiazolinone. All effective preservatives. Several of them are also documented endocrine disruptors or skin sensitizers, which is why label-reading customers avoid them.

organic_beeswax.name: Organic Beeswax
organic_beeswax.mechanism: Beeswax forms a breathable barrier on skin that locks moisture in without occluding pores the way petrolatum does. Its long-chain fatty acids and esters create a flexible film that holds skin's own water content while still allowing gas exchange. In lip balm it's the structural ingredient that lets the balm hold a stick shape and stay on the lips through wear.
organic_beeswax.sourcing: Organic beeswax from hives certified for organic apiculture practices — bees not exposed to synthetic pesticides or fed sugar syrup. Filtered but not bleached or deodorized.
organic_beeswax.why_we_chose_it: Petrolatum and microcrystalline wax are dirt-cheap and shelf-stable forever. They also fully occlude — they trap moisture but block skin's exchange, and microcrystalline wax is a petroleum byproduct. Beeswax does the moisture-lock job while remaining breathable, and customers can verify what it is.
organic_beeswax.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: Petrolatum (Vaseline), paraffin, microcrystalline wax, ozokerite. All petroleum-derived, all cheap, all create a more occlusive film than beeswax. The trade-off is the kind that's not visible until you spend years using them.

palm_stearic.name: Palm Stearic
palm_stearic.mechanism: Palm stearic acid is a saturated fatty acid that gives the body cream its rich, semi-solid texture without synthetic thickeners. It melts at body temperature and emulsifies with water-phase ingredients, allowing the cream to feel firm in the jar but spread and absorb on skin. The same fatty acid is what gives shea butter and cocoa butter their structural firmness.
palm_stearic.sourcing: Sustainably sourced palm stearic from RSPO-certified or equivalent supply chains. Plant-derived (vs. tallow-derived).
palm_stearic.why_we_chose_it: Stearyl alcohol or PEG-derived thickeners are cheaper to source consistently and easier to formulate with. We use palm stearic because it's recognizable as a plant fatty acid and delivers the texture we want for the cream — closer to a balm than a lotion. The trade-off is a less uniform melt point batch-to-batch.
palm_stearic.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: Stearyl alcohol (often petroleum-derived unless specified), PEG-100 stearate, polyacrylate-13, or carbomers. Cheaper, more shelf-stable, deliver the same thickness but with synthetic chemistry.

organic_red_palm_oil.name: Organic Red Palm Oil
organic_red_palm_oil.mechanism: Unrefined red palm oil retains its naturally high tocotrienol (a form of vitamin E) and beta-carotene content, both of which contribute antioxidant activity to the formula. Beta-carotene gives the oil its red-orange color — refined palm oil is bleached white and the carotenes are stripped out. The same antioxidant profile is what makes unrefined red palm oil a traditional cooking and skincare oil across West Africa.
organic_red_palm_oil.sourcing: Sustainably sourced organic red palm oil — RSPO-certified or equivalent. Cold-pressed and unrefined to retain the carotenoid and tocotrienol content. Color in the finished product reflects the unrefined oil, not added pigment.
organic_red_palm_oil.why_we_chose_it: Refined palm oil is dramatically cheaper, color-neutral, and shelf-stable. Refining strips out the carotenoids and tocotrienols. We use unrefined red palm oil because the antioxidants are why we're using palm oil at all.
organic_red_palm_oil.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: Refined, bleached, deodorized (RBD) palm oil. Identical fatty acid profile, none of the antioxidant content. Often appears simply as 'palm oil' on the back panel — different molecule, different work.

saponified_coconut_oil.name: Saponified Organic Virgin Coconut Oil
saponified_coconut_oil.mechanism: Saponification is the chemical reaction between an oil and a strong alkali (sodium hydroxide for bar soap, potassium hydroxide for liquid soap) that produces soap molecules and glycerin as byproducts. With coconut oil as the only fat, the resulting soap is high in lauric and capric acid soap chains — naturally lathering, naturally antimicrobial, recognizable as a true single-ingredient soap.
saponified_coconut_oil.sourcing: Organic virgin coconut oil (cold-pressed, unrefined) saponified in small batches with food-grade lye. The lye fully reacts during saponification — no free alkali remains in the finished bar.
saponified_coconut_oil.why_we_chose_it: Tallow (rendered beef fat) is the cheapest soap base by a wide margin and is the dominant fat in mass-market bar soap. We use coconut oil for the lather quality, the absence of animal fat, the lauric-acid antimicrobial property, and the single-ingredient simplicity. The customer who reads our label sees one fat name; the same customer reads commercial soap and sees a list.
saponified_coconut_oil.what_cheap_alternatives_look_like: Sodium tallowate (animal fat), sodium palmate (palm oil — often unsustainably sourced), or synthetic detergent bars (sodium lauryl sulfoacetate, sodium isethionate). All cheaper, all create a different kind of bar — animal-fat bars feel different on skin and the synthetic bars are technically not soap at all (they're solidified detergent).
